coredata.dc:title: High sensitivity detection of chromate (CrO4 2−) ions by using N, P-carbon dot-functionalized solution-gated graphene transistors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Zhanpeng
Song, Peng
Wang, Jianying
Dong, Junqi
Yu, Tingting
Gou, Yanzhuo
Wang, Xianbao
Li, Jinhua
coredata.dc:description: Chromate (CrO4 2−) ions, pervasive in industrial effluents and water sources, are potent carcinogens that can cause severe health issues like organ damage and cancer. Conventional detection methods for chromate ions are time-consuming and costly, with limited sensitivity, often failing to detect low concentrations for environmental and health safety. To address these issues, utilizing N and P doped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) as gate electrode probes, we have developed a new solution-gated graphene transistor (SGGT). The N, P-CDs, with a diameter of 4.2 nm, have rich functional groups like amino groups, carboxyl groups, and phosphate groups etc., which show the specific identification of CrO4 2− ions. The N, P-CDs are anchored onto the gold gate through mercaptoacetic acid (MAA). The CDs-SGGT sensors of CrO4 2− ions exhibit excellent selectivity, ultra-high sensitivity, and stability. The limit of detection (LOD) of sensors is as low as 2.69 × 10−19 M, outperforming conventional sensing methods. Furthermore, from 10−19 to 10−9 M, the sensors have a broad linear detection range. The N, P-CDs-SGGT sensor’s capability for real-time and real-sample detection of CrO4 2− ions offers a significant advancement in the field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Graphene
Transistor
Chromate ions
High selectivity
High sensitivity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N, P-CDs. (b) The emission and absorption spectra of N, P-CDs under 300 nm excitation. (c) Fourier transform infrared spectrum of N, P-CDs. (d) XPS spectrum of N, P-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence selectivity measurements of N, P-CDs. Seven anion ions, (Br−, Cl−, I−, MnO4 2−, NO3 −, OH−, and CrO4 2−) were tested. (b) Histogram of the fluorescence selectivity curves of the N, P-CDs for CrO4 2− ions. (c) PL spectra of the N, P-CDs at different CrO4 2− ions concentrations. (d) Relationship between (F 0 − F)/F 0 and Lg value of the CrO4 2− ions concentration.

(a) The gate (G), source (S), and drain (D) electrode of the SGGT device and the channel of graphene. (b) N, P-CDs functionalized gate. (c) Configuration of the CDs-SGGT sensor. (d) Schematic diagram of the capacitance changed mechanism of the gate surface before and after the CrO4 2− ions test.

(a) I D-V G curves tested in PBS solution for the different anions (Br−, Cl−, I−, MnO4 2−, NO3 −, OH−, and CrO4 2− ions). (b) The specificity measurement of sensors. (c) I D-V G curves tested in PBS solution before and after adding different concentrations of CrO4 2− ions. (d) The linear relationship of |ΔV Dirac| with the concentration of CrO4 2− ions.

(a) Real-time I D sensing of the sensor at different anions (Br−, Cl−, I−, MnO4 2−, NO3 −, OH−, and CrO4 2− ions). (b) Histogram of the selectivity curve. (c) Real-time I D of the device at various CrO4 2− ions concentration. (d) The relationship of |ΔI D| with the concentration of CrO4 2− ions.
(a) I D-V G curves tested before and after adding various concentrations of CrO4 2− ions in serum media. (b) The relationship of |ΔV Dirac| with the CrO4 2− ions concentration in serum media.

head.ce:title: High sensitivity detection of chromate (CrO4 2−) ions by using N, P-carbon dot-functionalized solution-gated graphene transistors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed by the Province and Ministry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of New Energy and Electrical Engineering, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials
Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed by the Province and Ministry
Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials
School of New Energy and Electrical Engineering
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed by the Province and Ministry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of New Energy and Electrical Engineering, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The N, P-CDs are utilized to fabricate a novel SGGT sensor for the detection of CrO4 2−. The sensors offer a cost-effective strategy for real-time, highly selective, and ultra-sensitive detection of CrO4 2− ions.

Chromate (CrO4 2−) ions, pervasive in industrial effluents and water sources, are potent carcinogens that can cause severe health issues like organ damage and cancer. Conventional detection methods for chromate ions are time-consuming and costly, with limited sensitivity, often failing to detect low concentrations for environmental and health safety. To address these issues, utilizing N and P doped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) as gate electrode probes, we have developed a new solution-gated graphene transistor (SGGT). The N, P-CDs, with a diameter of 4.2 nm, have rich functional groups like amino groups, carboxyl groups, and phosphate groups etc., which show the specific identification of CrO4 2− ions. The N, P-CDs are anchored onto the gold gate through mercaptoacetic acid (MAA). The CDs-SGGT sensors of CrO4 2− ions exhibit excellent selectivity, ultra-high sensitivity, and stability. The limit of detection (LOD) of sensors is as low as 2.69 × 10−19 M, outperforming conventional sensing methods. Furthermore, from 10−19 to 10−9 M, the sensors have a broad linear detection range. The N, P-CDs-SGGT sensor’s capability for real-time and real-sample detection of CrO4 2− ions offers a significant advancement in the field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium is ranked as the seventh most abundant element on Earth. According to its valence state, chromium compounds play different roles in industry [1], manufacturing, and the environment [2]. Chromium mainly has two forms of Cr3+ and Cr6+ [3], where trivalent chromium is an essential trace element beneficial to human health, while hexavalent chromium is widely concerned due to its high toxicity and carcinogenicity. Cr6+ is commonly found in many chromates, predominantly as the chromate ions (CrO4 2−) that present in compounds like potassium chromate and ammonium chromate [4]. Chromite (Cr3+) can be oxidized to highly toxic Cr6+ ions in the body, which can interact with DNA, causing serious damage to cells and tissues [5,6]. Cr6+ ions can cause damage to the respiratory system, lungs, and liver, and increase the risk of cancer [7]. In addition, Cr6+ ions can pollute water and soil, causing impacts on ecosystems [8]. Therefore, it is important to detect the Cr6+ ions regularly. By detecting the presence and concentration of Cr6+ ions, potential health risks in the workplace, environment or other scenarios can be assessed and are critical to protecting human health.

Currently, various methods are employed for the detection of CrO4 2− ions, including colorimetry [9], fluorometry [2,10,11], atomic absorption spectrometry [12], liquid chromatography [13], mass spectrometry [14], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [15], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [16], X-ray techniques [17], and electrochemistry [18]. For instance, Li et al. developed a colorimetric method utilizing chromate oxidation to etch Cu@Au nanoparticles for the detection of CrO4 2− ions [9]. This method exhibits a LOD of 0.21 nM and detection range of 0.4 nM to 1 μM for CrO4 2−. Liu et al. synthesized a hydrolytically stable cationic luminescent europium (III) metal–organic framework (MOF), serving as a luminescence sensor with rapid and selective fluorescence quenching properties for CrO4 2− ions [2]. The sensor has a LOD of 0.56 ppb for CrO4 2− ions in deionized water and a detection range of 0.2 ppm to 400 ppm. Given the limitations of conventional methods for detecting CrO4 2− ions, such as reliance on large instruments, complex procedures, specialized operator, time-consumption, and lack of real-time monitoring capability. It is still a challange for developing new technologies for rapid, precise, selective, and sensitive detection of CrO4 2− ions.

To realize an improved ion detection method, the solution-gated graphene transistors (SGGTs) are developed as biochemical sensors. SGGTs present several advantages, such as high sensitivity, low power consumption, easy integration, good biocompatibility, and cost-effectiveness [19–21]. The SGGT sensors have been used to detect a variety of analytes, including ions [22–26], small molecules [27,28], proteins [19,29], DNA [29–31] and RNA [32]. Among the probes of the SGGT sensor, carbon dots (CDs), as a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, offer exceptional properties such as excellent biocompatibility, a distinctive surface structure, and abundant surface functional groups. These attributes enable CDs an ideal probe for SGGT sensors. We find that introducing CDs into SGGT technology will enhance the sensitivity and selectivity of ion detection, contributing to the overall effectiveness of the detection method [24–26]. Our group develop a novel CDs-functionalized SGGT (CDs-SGGT) sensor for the detection of Fe3+, [25] Cu2+ [24], and Co2+ ions [26]. The sensors exhibit impressive LOD for Fe3+, Cu2+, and Co2+ ions at 0.1 fM, 10 fM, and 0.1 aM, respectively, with detection ranges spanning from 0.1 fM-1 μM, 10 fM-mM, and 0.1 aM-1 fM, respectively. To date, The SGGT sensor has excelled in cation detection, however, its potential in anion detection, especially, CrO4 2− ions, remains unexplored.

In this study, we develop an N, P-doped CDs probe functionalized SGGT sensor to elucidate the mechanism for anion detection, specifically targeting CrO4 2− ions. The N, P-CDs-SGGT sensor exhibited remarkable sensitivity. Its LOD is as low as 10−19 M. The linear detection is ranging from 10−19 to 10−9 M. Additionally, the CDs-SGGT sensor displays excellent selectivity for CrO4 2− ions. The N, P-CDs probe efficiently captures CrO4 2− ions, inducing a noticeable Dirac voltage shift (ΔV Dirac) in the SGGT transfer curve. The variation in ΔV Dirac induces a corresponding change in the channel current (ΔI D), enabling real-time CrO4 2− ions detection through the measurement of ΔI D. Importantly, the practical serum sample detection has been realized.

Fig. 1 a shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the synthesized N, P-CDs. The average size of the N, P-CDs is approximately 4.2 nm. The N, P-CDs exhibit a sphere-like morphology and good dispersion. The N, P-CDs also exhibit an amorphous morphology. Fig. 1b presents the ultraviolet absorption and emission spectra of the N, P-CDs under 300 nm excitation. The absorption peak is observed at 279 nm, coming from the π–π* transition [33]. Correspondingly, the fluorescence emission is at 327 nm. These results highlight the distinctive optical properties of the N, P-CDs. Fig. S1 shows the excitation dependence of N, P-CDs at various excitation wavelengths. It is observed that the photoluminescence (PL) peak undergoes a redshift as the excitation wavelength increases. Additionally, the N, P-CDs exhibit the highest fluorescence emission intensity when excited at a wavelength of 300 nm. Fig. 1c and d illustrate the analysis of the surface structure of the N, P-CDs using Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), respectively. The results show that the wide absorption band near 3420 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of N–H/O–H. The characteristic area of 1580–1398 cm−1 shows three typical vibration peaks: 1643 cm−1 (vibration of the C=O/C=N skeleton), 1512 cm−1 (aromatic C=C conjugated system), and 1398 cm−1 (symmetrical stretching of the C–P/C–N bond); In the phosphorus characteristic region of 1200–900 cm−1, the two characteristic peaks at 1180 cm−1 and 1110 cm−1 corresponds to P–O stretching and P–OH bending vibration, respectively; The absorption peak at 496 cm−1 is attributed to the asymmetric vibration of PO4 3−. The above IR absorption peaks can also be confirmed by the previous literature [34]. The presence of nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) doping in the N, P-CDs is confirmed by the XPS analysis. The XPS spectrum reveals four peaks at 280.9 eV, 399.1 eV, 529.9 eV, and 131.8 eV, corresponding to the C1s, N1s, O1s, and P2p orbitals, respectively. Additionally, the XPS spectrum of the C1s orbital, as depicted in Fig. S2a, shows three Gaussian peaks at 286.6 eV, 284.7 eV, and 288.6 eV, corresponding to the C=P/C=N, C–C/C–P, and COOH groups, respectively. The spectrum of N1s exhibits two Gaussian peaks, located at 402.2 eV and 400.4 eV, attributed to the N–C=O and N–(C)3 groups, respectively, as depicted in Fig. S2b. Additionally, the XPS spectrum of O1s displays a single peak at 532.4 eV, attributed to the C=O group, as shown in Fig. S2c. Furthermore, the P2p spectrum of the N, P-CDs reveals a peak at 134.6 eV, corresponding to the P-C group, as illustrated in Fig. S2d. Combining the FTIR and XPS analysis, it is evident that the N, P-CDs surface is rich in amino, carboxyl, and phosphate groups. These functional groups play crucial roles in determining the CDs chemical reactivity and surface properties.

Fig. 2 a shows the fluorescence intensity curve of N, P-CDs on various anions, including Br−, Cl−, I−, MnO4 2−, NO3 −, OH−, and CrO4 2− ions. These measurements were conducted at an ion concentration of 10−5 M. Among the tested anions, CrO4 2− ions exhibits the most significant fluorescence attenuation effect on N, P-CDs, followed by MnO4 2− ions. Additionally, the fluorescence attenuation caused by other ions such as Br−, Cl−, I−, NO3 −, and OH− is less than one-fifth of that observed for CrO4 2− ions, as shown in Fig. 2b. This shows that N, P-CDs have excellent fluorescence selectivity for CrO4 2− and MnO4 2− ions, simultaneously. The highest selectivity to CrO4 2− ions probably come from the inner filter effect [35]. The fluorescence sensitivity of CrO4 2− ions was investigated by using N, P-CDs as a fluorescence sensor, as depicted in Fig. 2c. The fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs decreases with increasing concentrations of CrO4 2− ions. In Fig. 2d, the logarithmic value of CrO4 2− ions concentration (LgC) is plotted on the horizontal axis, while the percentage of fluorescence decay intensity ((F 0 − F)/F 0) of N, P-CDs is plotted on the vertical axis. The correlation between these two variables, (F 0 − F)/F 0 and LgC, demonstrates a relatively weak linear relationship, which probably come from the weak specific interaction during the complexation of chromate ions with the N, P-CDs at low metal ion concentrations. The CrO4 2− ions concentration can be determined by the calibration Eq. (1):
(F 0 − F)/F 0 = 0.22LgC + 1.71(1)
the correlation coefficient R 2 = 0.915. Where, F 0 and F is the fluorescence intensity before and after adding CrO4 2− ions. From 10−8 to 10−3 M, the sensors show a linear fluorescence detection of CrO4 2− ions. Based on N, P-CDs good selectivity and sensitivity for CrO4 2− ions, we then utilized them as probes to alter the gate surface of the SGGT in order to create a CDs-SGGT sensor specifically designed for the detection of CrO4 2− ions.

Fig. 3 a illustrates the fabrication process of a SGGT. This process involves patterning gold (Au) onto a glass substrate, followed by transferring graphene onto the channel using a wet transfer method. The detailed methodology for this process is outlined in experimental section in ESI. In Fig. 3b, the modification process of N, P-CDs on the gate electrode begins with the formation of an Au-S bond through the self-assembly method. The thioglycolic acid (MAA) is modified on the gate surface. Then, the carboxylic groups of MAA on the gate are activated by EDC/NHS solution, and the N, P-CDs are fixed on the gate surface. To explore the sensing mechanism of the sensor, we apply voltages to three electrodes. The entire test process is carried out in a PBS solution (pH = 7.2) in the PDMS well, as shown in Fig. 3c. The sensing mechanism is illustrated in Fig. S3 and Fig. 3d. When V G is applied, electric double layers (EDLs) form at both the channel/electrolyte interface and the gate/electrolyte interface. Upon introducing CrO4 2− ions solution into the PDMS well, the N, P-CDs probe on the gate surface traps the CrO4 2− ions. This process increases the distance between the gate electrode and the negatively charged ions, resulting in a thicker EDL. So, the thickness of the EDL near the gate electrode will increase. The capacitance of the device can be expressed by the following equations.
(2)
C ∝
1 d
(3)
1 C
=
1
C
G - E
+
1
C
E - C
where C is capacitance, d is the thickness of the EDL, C G-E and C E-C are the capacitors of the two EDLs at the gate/electrolyte and electrolyte/graphene interfaces, respectively. From Eqs. (2) and (3), an increase in EDL thickness near the gate causes a decrease in C G-E. Consequently, the total capacitance C of the entire SGGT sensor will decrease. We conducted capacitance testing on the sensor before and after the experiment, as depicted in Fig. S4. The findings reveal a decrease in the capacitance of the entire sensor following the introduction of CrO4 2− ions, consistent with the results in Fig. 3d. Fig. S3 illustrates the variation in gate and drain potential before and after the capture of CrO4 2− ions. Upon the capture of CrO4 2− ions by N, P-CDs on the gate, the potential undergoes redistribution owing to the modification in capacitance near the gate electrode.

To investigate the selectivity of the sensor, we detected CrO4 2− ions and other six anions including Br−, Cl−, I−, MnO4 2−, NO3 − and OH− ions. PBS was utilized as a control sample. As shown in Fig. 4 a, there is an obvious Dirac voltage shift (ΔV Dirac) for CrO4 2− ions. Other six anions of the same concentration cause a small amount of the ΔV Dirac. In these interference ions tests, the maximum ΔV Dirac is 0.02 V. Nevertheless, the ΔV Dirac of the CrO4 2− ions surpasses 0.02 V by threefold, as depicted in Fig. 4b. The results indicate the SGGT sensor shows excellent specificity. As demonstrated in Fig. S5, the control experiment without N, P-CDs modification has been conducted. The results reveal negligible ΔV Dirac for all anions. This implies that the response of the sensor to CrO4 2− ions is attributed to N, P-CDs modification on the gate electrode of the SGGT. Fig. 4c shows the sensor transfer curves at the various concentrations of CrO4 2− ions. The curve depicts bipolar transfer characteristics [32]. As the concentration of CrO4 2− ions in the PDMS well increases from 10−19 to 10−9 M, the transfer curve of the V Dirac shifts towards positive voltage. This phenomenon occurs because the CrO4 2− ions bind to the N, P-CDs, leading to a decrease in gate capacitance. According to the equations, channel current I D changes with V G,
(4)
I D
≈
W L
μ
C i
V G
-
V Dirac
V DS
V G
-
V Dirac
> >
V DS
where V DS and V G are the drain and gate voltages respectively, W and L are the width and length of the channel, respectively, μ is the mobility of graphene carriers (electrons or holes), C i is the gate capacitance, and V Dirac is the voltage at the charge neutral point. If the device’s I D remains constant, according to equations (4), a lower V G is necessary when the C i decreases. Consequently, as the concentration of CrO4 2− ions increases, the transfer curve of the device will shift towards positive gate voltage. Further, the quantitative correlation between ΔV Dirac and CrO4 2− ions concentration was also investigated. As depicted in Fig. 4d, ΔV Dirac exhibits a highly linear fit within the concentration of CrO4 2− ions in range from 10−19 to 10−9 M, with a R 2 value of 0.999. This suggests a robust linear relationship between |ΔV Dirac| of SSGT and the logarithm of the ion concentration of CrO4 2− ions. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated using the formula:
(5)
LOD=
3
Δ V
noise
-a
b
where ΔV noise denotes the background noise signal of the blank device, a and b are the intercept and slope of the calibration linear relation. When the signal of the targets is three times the background noise, the signal is considered to be reliable. From Fig. 4c and d, it’s evident that the sensor’s LOD can achieve 2.69 × 10−19 M. Furthermore, stability tests conducted on the N, P-CDs-SGGT sensor, as illustrated in Fig. S6. The I D-V G curve of the device was repeated three times without the introduction of ions. Notably, the size of the error bars suggests excellent stability of this sensor. This robust stability is crucial for reliable and long-term sensor performance, ensuring consistent and accurate measurements over time.

Fig. 5 a illustrates the real-time selectivity exhibited by the CDs-SGGT sensor towards CrO4 2−ions. Various anions including Br−, Cl−, I−, MnO4 2−, NO3 −, OH−, and CrO4 2− ions were selected for comparing I D response levels. The corresponding I D-V G curve of the sensor is provided in Fig. S7. The findings indicate that among the tested anions, the sensor exhibits the highest current response to CrO4 2− ions. This selectivity profile is valuable for accurately detecting and distinguishing CrO4 2− ions from other interfering species, enhancing the sensor's utility in practical applications. Fig. 5b highlights that the |ΔI D| for CrO4 2− is at least 5 times greater than that of the other six tested anions. This substantial difference underscores the robust real-time selectivity towards CrO4 2− ions. Additionally, Fig. 5c presents real-time I D sensing towards CrO4 2− ions ranging from 10−19 to 10−9 M. Fig. S8 presents the I D-V G curve corresponding to Fig. 5c. Upon the introduction of CrO4 2− ions into the PDMS well, the I D initially decreases and eventually stabilizes after the capture of CrO4 2− ions by the N, P-CDs. Notably, the decrease in I D correlates with an increase in CrO4 2− ions concentration, aligning with the displacement observed in the transfer curve. This behavior suggests that the sensor’s I D response is proportional to the logarithm value of CrO4 2− concentration of, further validating its efficacy for quantitative detection applications. The device demonstrates a notable current response even at a concentration as low as 10−19 M for CrO4 2− ions. This ultralow concentration corresponds to the sensor’s LOD, which reaches 10−19 M. Remarkably, this LOD is lower than those reported previously, as evidenced by Table S1. Moreover, by plotting and linearly fitting |ΔI D| and the concentration of the CrO4 2− ions, the linear relationship can be observed, as depicted in Fig. 5d. This linear correlation further strengthens the sensor's potential for precise quantification of CrO4 2− ions across a wide concentration range. The strong linear relationship with a R 2 value of 0.987 is evident, underscoring the reliability of the sensor's response across the concentration range. Fig. S9 shows the SEM images and mapped images of the gate surfaces before and after CrO4 2− ion testing. Notably, the gate electrode surface morphology exhibits the distinct difference before and after the test. Prior to detection, the gate surface displays elements such as C, O, N, and P, indicating successful fixation of the N, P-CDs on the gate surface. After detection, the presence of Cr elements on the gate surface is notable. It suggests that the N, P-CDs probe initially captures the CrO4 2− ions, which subsequently accumulate on the gate surface. These findings provide further validation that the CDs can entrap CrO4 2− ions, establishing their utility as probes for SGGT sensors designed for CrO4 2− ions sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of CrO4 2− ions in serum media
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, the N, P-CDs-SGGT sensor is employed to accurately determine the concentrations of CrO4 2− ions ions in artificially spiked serum samples. Fig. 6 a shows the sensors I D-V G curves before and after adding various concentrations of CrO4 2− ions in serum media. As the concentration of CrO4 2− ions escalates in serum samples, a pronounced shift in the V Dirac towards the positive gate voltage direction is observed. This positive correlation is in harmony with the previous findings on CrO4 2− ions detection conducted in the absence of serum samples, reinforcing the consistency and reliability of the detection method (Fig. 4). Additionally, from the relationship of |ΔV Dirac| and the CrO4 2− ions concentration, a linear curve is obtained (R 2 = 0.993), as depicted in Fig. 6b. Therefore, the sensor can be successfully employed to detect CrO4 2− ions in serum media. The sensor exhibits a wide sensing range of 10−19 M−10−9 M for CrO4 2− ions in serum media. A LOD is as low as 10−19 M. Thus, this sensor can be potentially applied in health detection.

In summary, we prepared a novel N, P-CDs-SGGT sensor for the determination of CrO4 2− ions. The N, P-CDs serve as the probes modified on the gate surface of the SGGT sensor. The N, P-CDs-SGGT sensors offer a cost-effective solution for real-time, highly selective, and ultra-sensitive detection of CrO4 2− ions. Notably, the sensor achieves an impressive LOD at 2.69 × 10−19 M, with a linear detection range spanning from 10−19 to 10−9 M. The sensing mechanism of the sensor may come from the displacement of the transfer curve, which occurs due to the alteration of the EDL caused by the trapping of CrO4 2− ions by the CDs probe on the gate electrode. The CDs-SGGT sensors possess a simple structure, exhibiting excellent integration and stability. These features render it suitable for development into portable detection equipment of CrO4 2− ions detection for healthcare monitor.

We declare that all authors are free from conflicts of interest that could affect the objectivity and impartiality of the findings. We guarantee that all data and results of this study are accurate and objective and free from any external influence.

Zhanpeng Ren: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Peng Song: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jianying Wang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Junqi Dong: Methodology, Data curation. Tingting Yu: Methodology, Investigation. Yanzhuo Gou: Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xianbao Wang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Jinhua Li: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Hubei Provincial Department of Science & Technology (2020BIB020), Knowledge Innovation Program of Wuhan Science and Technology Bureau (Grant No. 2023020201010139) and the Overseas Expertise Introduction Center for Discipline Innovation (D18025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.114114.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in adsorptive removal and catalytic reduction of hexavalent chromium by metal–organic frameworks composites
J. Mol. Liq.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.E. Mahmoud, S.M. Elsayed, S.E.M. Mahmoud, R.O. Aljedaani, M.A. Salam, Recent advances in adsorptive removal and catalytic reduction of hexavalent chromium by metal–organic frameworks composites, J. Mol. Liq. 347 (2022) 118274.
